questions.explanation: Isaac Newton (1643-1727), English physicist and Lucasian Professor at Cambridge, published Philosophiae Naturalis Principia Mathematica in 1687—the most influential physics book ever written—deriving three laws of motion and the law of universal gravitation F = G m1 m2 / r^2. He showed that the same force pulling an apple down ALSO holds the Moon in orbit. Newton co-invented calculus (with Leibniz) and built the first reflecting telescope. NYS Grade 6 Science (NGSS MS-ESS1-3) requires crediting Newton 1687 with the gravitational law.

questions.prompt: Path one object follows around another due to gravity, like Earth around the Sun.
questions.explanation: An orbit is the gravitationally bound path of one object around another. Johannes Kepler showed in 1609 (Astronomia Nova) that orbits are ellipses with the central mass at one focus—NOT circles. The Moon's orbit, Earth's orbit, and satellite orbits are all ellipses. Circular orbits are special cases. Newton's 1687 derivation from the inverse-square law explained why Kepler's empirical laws held. NYS Grade 6 Science (NGSS MS-ESS1-3) requires students to define orbit, credit Kepler 1609 with elliptical orbits, and link Newton 1687 as the theoretical explanation.

questions.prompt: Force that pulls objects on Earth's surface downward at 9.8 meters per second squared.
questions.explanation: Earth's surface gravity is approximately 9.8 m/s^2, meaning a falling object speeds up by 9.8 m/s every second of fall. Galileo Galilei established this constant by dropping objects from the Tower of Pisa (legendary) and rolling balls down inclined planes around 1604, showing all objects fall at the same rate (in vacuum) regardless of mass. The value of g varies slightly with altitude and latitude (poles ~9.83, equator ~9.78). NYS Grade 6 Science (NGSS MS-ESS1-3) requires the 9.8 m/s^2 value and Galileo's free-fall experiments.

questions.prompt: Apparent loss of weight astronauts feel in orbit because they are continuously falling around Earth.
questions.explanation: Microgravity (often called weightlessness or zero-g) is NOT the absence of gravity—the ISS at 400 km altitude still feels about 89% of surface gravity. Astronauts feel weightless because they are in continuous free-fall, falling at the same rate as their spacecraft. Albert Einstein recognized this equivalence in 1907 as a foundation of general relativity. Sustained microgravity causes bone loss and muscle atrophy. NYS Grade 6 Science (NGSS MS-ESS1-3) requires students to recognize that orbit is a free-fall state, not gravity absence.

questions.prompt: Year humans first walked on the Moon, as part of NASA's Apollo 11 mission.
questions.explanation: On July 20, 1969, Neil Armstrong and Buzz Aldrin walked on the Moon during NASA's Apollo 11 mission while Michael Collins orbited above in the Command Module Columbia. Armstrong's first words: 'That's one small step for [a] man, one giant leap for mankind.' Apollo 11 was the climax of John F. Kennedy's 1961 challenge to put a man on the Moon by decade's end. Six Apollo missions landed 12 astronauts on the Moon 1969-1972. NYS Grade 6 Science (NGSS MS-ESS1-3) requires students to identify 1969 and Apollo 11.

questions.prompt: Robotic vehicles such as Curiosity and Perseverance that explore the surface of Mars.
questions.explanation: Mars rovers are robotic vehicles exploring Mars's surface. Sojourner (1997) was first. Spirit and Opportunity (2004-2018) found chemical evidence of past liquid water. Curiosity (landed 2012, still operating) tested habitability in Gale Crater. Perseverance (landed February 18, 2021) is collecting samples for a future Mars Sample Return and carried the Ingenuity helicopter—the first flight on another planet. NYS Grade 6 Science (NGSS MS-ESS1-3) requires students to name rovers, identify NASA's role, and explain what evidence they seek about Mars's habitability.

questions.prompt: 1990 telescope orbiting Earth that has imaged distant galaxies and the early universe.
questions.explanation: The Hubble Space Telescope launched April 24, 1990 aboard Space Shuttle Discovery. Named for Edwin Hubble, it orbits at 540 km altitude, above atmospheric blurring, with a 2.4-meter primary mirror. Its Deep Field images (1995, 2004) revealed galaxies as they existed 13 billion years ago. After 1993 servicing fixed its mirror flaw, it has been astronomy's flagship instrument for over 30 years. NYS Grade 6 Science (NGSS MS-ESS1-2) requires students to identify the Hubble Space Telescope, its 1990 launch, and its role imaging galaxies far back in cosmic time.

questions.prompt: 2021 infrared telescope at the L2 Lagrange point that sees the oldest galaxies.
questions.explanation: The James Webb Space Telescope launched December 25, 2021 on an Ariane 5 rocket and arrived at the Sun-Earth L2 Lagrange point in January 2022. Its 6.5-meter segmented gold-coated mirror collects infrared light, allowing it to see galaxies forming just 200 million years after the Big Bang. JWST is a NASA-ESA-CSA collaboration, costing about $10 billion and taking 25 years to build. Released first images July 12, 2022. NYS Grade 6 Science (NGSS MS-ESS1-2) requires students to identify JWST as Hubble's infrared successor.

questions.prompt: Lagrange point one of five gravitational equilibrium spots in the Earth-Sun system.
questions.explanation: L2 is one of five Lagrange points in the Earth-Sun system, located 1.5 million km from Earth on the night side—along the line Sun-Earth-L2. Joseph-Louis Lagrange derived the five gravitational points in 1772. At L2 a small object orbits the Sun in lockstep with Earth, hidden in Earth's shadow from solar interference. JWST (launched 2021), the Planck satellite (2009-2013), and the WMAP probe (2001-2010) all orbited L2 for stable cold conditions and clear infrared views. NYS Grade 6 Science (NGSS MS-ESS1-3) honors Lagrange 1772.

questions.prompt: First step in the engineering design process where you state what the problem is.
questions.explanation: Defining the problem is the first step in any engineering design process: state what the problem is, who has it, and why it matters. NGSS MS-ETS1-1 requires that students 'define the criteria and constraints' clearly before designing anything. Henry Petroski's 1985 book To Engineer Is Human emphasizes that most engineering failures trace back to poorly defined problems. NYS Grade 6 Science requires students to write a one-sentence problem statement, identify stakeholders, and distinguish a real engineering problem (a need) from a wish.

questions.prompt: Specific requirements a solution must meet, such as 'cost less than $20' or 'fit in a backpack.'
questions.explanation: Criteria are the specific requirements a successful solution must meet—what it must DO. Examples: 'support 5 kg of weight,' 'fit in a 30 cm cube,' 'cost under $20,' 'operate at temperatures from -10 C to 40 C.' Criteria are measurable. NGSS MS-ETS1-1 requires that students 'define the criteria and constraints of a design problem with sufficient precision' BEFORE designing solutions. NYS Grade 6 Science assessments test whether students can extract criteria from a scenario prompt and write them as testable requirements.

questions.prompt: Limits on a design, such as available materials, time, or money, sometimes called restrictions.
questions.explanation: Constraints are the limits within which a solution must work—boundaries on materials, budget, time, weight, energy, safety, or environmental impact. NGSS MS-ETS1-1 lists constraints as a co-equal requirement with criteria. Real-world examples: NASA's Apollo lunar module had a strict mass constraint (15,000 kg); modern smartphones face battery and screen-size constraints. NYS Grade 6 Science requires students to distinguish criteria (what the solution must do) from constraints (limits on how it can be built).

questions.prompt: Group of people the solution should serve, such as elementary students or visually impaired users.
questions.explanation: End users are the people the design ultimately serves—children, athletes, doctors, soldiers, factory workers, or specific subgroups like the visually impaired or wheelchair users. Identifying end users early in problem definition prevents disasters: the Ford Pinto (1971) ignored rear-impact-safety needs, leading to lawsuits and recalls. Universal Design (Ronald Mace, 1985) emphasizes designing FOR ALL users including those with disabilities. NYS Grade 6 Science (NGSS MS-ETS1-1) requires students to name target end users before designing.

questions.prompt: Bigger-picture problem 'How can we reduce plastic waste in oceans?' is an example of this kind.
questions.explanation: Global challenges are large-scale societal problems—plastic pollution, climate change, antibiotic resistance, water scarcity, energy access, food security—that engineering can help address but cannot solve alone. The US National Academy of Engineering announced its Grand Challenges for Engineering in 2008, listing 14 priorities such as 'provide energy from fusion' and 'make solar energy economical.' NGSS MS-ETS1-1 expects students to recognize that grand-challenge problems must be broken into smaller, well-defined sub-problems.

questions.prompt: Stage where teams generate many possible ideas without judging them right away.
questions.explanation: Brainstorming, named by ad executive Alex Osborn in his 1953 book Applied Imagination, is a creative-thinking technique where teams generate many ideas while suspending judgment. Osborn's four rules: defer judgment, encourage wild ideas, build on others' ideas, go for quantity. Studies (Diehl and Stroebe 1987) show individuals brainstorming alone first, then pooling, often outperform pure group brainstorming. NGSS MS-ETS1-2 requires students to brainstorm multiple solutions before choosing one. NYS Grade 6 Science assessments expect named brainstorming as a design step.

questions.prompt: Hand-drawn picture used to record an idea quickly so others can see what you mean.
questions.explanation: A sketch is a quick freehand drawing capturing a design idea fast enough to keep up with brainstorming. Leonardo da Vinci's late-1400s notebooks are the classic example—thousands of pages of mechanical, anatomical, and architectural sketches. NGSS MS-ETS1-2 requires students to externalize ideas in drawings before building. NYS Grade 6 Science assessments often ask students to provide a labeled sketch showing materials and key dimensions, demonstrating that drawing is a critical engineering thinking tool—not just art.

questions.prompt: Phase where engineers compare ideas against criteria and pick the most promising one.
questions.explanation: Evaluation is the design step where teams compare brainstormed solutions against the criteria and constraints from problem definition. Common tools include decision matrices (Pugh charts, developed by Stuart Pugh in 1981) that rate each option against each criterion. NGSS MS-ETS1-2 ('evaluate competing design solutions using a systematic process') and MS-ETS1-3 ('analyze data from tests to determine similarities and differences among several design solutions') both require this. NYS Grade 6 Science assessments test decision-matrix literacy.

questions.prompt: Working preliminary model of a design used to test ideas in the real world.
questions.explanation: A prototype is a working preliminary version built to test ideas. Cardboard, 3D-printed plastic, foam, and breadboarded circuits are common prototype materials. The Wright brothers' 1900-1902 glider prototypes preceded their 1903 powered flight; Steve Jobs's 2007 iPhone went through hundreds of foam prototypes. NGSS MS-ETS1-4 requires students to 'develop a model to generate data for iterative testing'—a prototype IS that model. NYS Grade 6 Science assessments expect a labeled prototype photo and test plan.

questions.prompt: Discipline like materials science or computer science from which engineers draw ideas.
questions.explanation: Engineering draws on every science discipline: civil engineers use materials science and geology, electrical engineers use physics, biomedical engineers use biology and chemistry. Engineering applies scientific knowledge to design solutions; science seeks to UNDERSTAND nature, engineering seeks to USE that understanding. NGSS frames this as the science-engineering loop: investigations feed designs, designs feed new investigations. NYS Grade 6 Science (NGSS MS-ETS1-1) requires students to identify the science behind a chosen engineering approach.

questions.prompt: Carefully planned procedure to see if a prototype meets the design criteria.
questions.explanation: A test is a controlled procedure to measure whether a prototype meets each design criterion—weight capacity, speed, accuracy, cost, durability. Tests should have a clear protocol, defined success metric, and repeatable conditions. The Wright brothers ran thousands of wind-tunnel tests in 1901 before their Kitty Hawk flight. NGSS MS-ETS1-3 requires students to 'analyze data from tests... to determine similarities and differences'—data from tests guides iteration. NYS Grade 6 Science assessments expect documented test procedures.

questions.prompt: Process of repeating design, build, test, and revise cycles to improve a solution.
questions.explanation: Iteration is the heart of engineering—repeating design, build, test, and revise cycles to improve a solution. The Wright brothers iterated their wing camber, propeller shape, and elevator placement from 1900 to 1903. SpaceX deliberately tests rockets to failure (Starship 2019-2024) to learn fast. NGSS MS-ETS1-4 requires students to use 'iterative testing and modification' to develop their design. NYS Grade 6 Science assessments expect a written record showing at least one revision from prototype v1 to v2.

questions.prompt: What changes from one trial to the next while everything else stays the same.
questions.explanation: A variable is the one parameter changed in a test while everything else (controls) stays constant—wing length, propeller pitch, battery capacity. Francis Bacon emphasized this controlled-experiment idea in his 1620 Novum Organum. Changing only ONE variable per test lets engineers isolate cause and effect. NGSS Science and Engineering Practice 3 (Planning and Carrying Out Investigations) requires identifying and controlling variables. NYS Grade 6 Science assessments grade students on whether they tested one variable at a time.

questions.prompt: Process of pinpointing what's wrong when something doesn't work.
questions.explanation: Troubleshooting (debugging in software) is the systematic process of finding the cause of a failure: isolate variables, check each subsystem, test hypotheses. The term 'debugging' originated in 1947 when Grace Hopper's team found an actual moth in Harvard's Mark II relay computer. Engineers use fault trees, root-cause analysis (the '5 Whys' technique, developed by Sakichi Toyoda for Toyota), and signal tracing. NGSS MS-ETS1-3 requires students to analyze test data to identify failure modes. NYS Grade 6 Science requires troubleshooting documentation.

questions.prompt: Final design choice picked because it best meets all criteria and constraints.
questions.explanation: An optimized solution is the design picked after several iterations because it best satisfies ALL criteria and constraints—not just one. Optimization often involves trade-offs: lighter weight may cost more, stronger may be heavier. The Pareto frontier (Vilfredo Pareto 1906) describes the boundary of best trade-offs. NGSS MS-ETS1-3 requires students to 'modify a proposed object, tool, or process such that an optimal design can be achieved.' NYS Grade 6 Science assessments expect a final-design defense citing trade-offs.

questions.prompt: Metal that bends easily, conducts electricity, and is used for wires.
questions.explanation: Copper (atomic number 29, symbol Cu) is a malleable, ductile metal with the second-highest electrical conductivity of any pure element (after silver). Humans have used copper for about 10,000 years (Copper Age, beginning ~8000 BCE). It is the standard material for electrical wiring, plumbing pipes, and motor windings because of its conductivity and corrosion resistance. NYS Grade 6 Science (NGSS MS-ETS1-2) asks students to choose copper for circuit projects, citing electrical conductivity. Aluminum and silver are alternatives—silver is more conductive but expensive.

questions.prompt: Material that resists electric current and is used to coat wires for safety.
questions.explanation: An insulator resists electrical current flow. Common insulators: rubber, plastic, glass, dry wood, porcelain. Electrical wire is coated with PVC or rubber insulators to prevent shock and short circuits. The opposite is a conductor (copper, aluminum). Semiconductors (silicon, germanium) fall between and form the basis of modern electronics—William Shockley, John Bardeen, and Walter Brattain invented the silicon transistor at Bell Labs in 1947 (Nobel 1956). NYS Grade 6 Science (NGSS MS-ETS1-2) tests identifying conductors vs. insulators in circuits.

questions.prompt: Property describing how strong a material is against breaking when pulled.
questions.explanation: Tensile strength is the maximum stress a material can withstand while being pulled before breaking, measured in pascals (Pa) or megapascals (MPa). Structural steel has 400-1860 MPa, spider silk 1300 MPa, Kevlar 3620 MPa, and carbon fiber over 4000 MPa. Robert Hooke discovered the linear stress-strain law in 1660 (Hooke's law: F = -kx). Engineers select materials for bridges, ropes, and elevator cables based on tensile strength. NYS Grade 6 Science (NGSS MS-ETS1-2) tests material-selection reasoning based on strength data.

questions.prompt: Tool that holds and turns screws to fasten parts together.
questions.explanation: A screwdriver turns screws to fasten parts. The Phillips-head screwdriver was patented in 1936 by Henry F. Phillips, allowing automatic assembly-line driving without slipping. Robertson square-drive (Peter Robertson 1908) is even better but never widely adopted in the US. Hex and Torx are modern variants. Engineers select fasteners (screws, bolts, rivets) based on load, vibration tolerance, and disassembly needs. NYS Grade 6 Science (NGSS MS-ETS1-2) expects students to know basic tools and reasoning behind fastener choice.

questions.prompt: 3-D printer technique that builds objects by laying down thin layers of melted plastic.
questions.explanation: Fused Deposition Modeling (FDM) builds objects by extruding thermoplastic filament layer by layer. S. Scott Crump invented and patented FDM in 1989, founding Stratasys to commercialize it. After his patent expired in 2009, the open-source RepRap project (Adrian Bowyer 2005) democratized FDM—home printers under $300 now exist. Engineers use FDM for rapid prototyping. NYS Grade 6 Science (NGSS MS-ETS1-4) recognizes 3D printing as a prototype-iteration tool letting students test designs faster than ever.

questions.prompt: Bridge type using cables hanging from towers that includes the Golden Gate.
questions.explanation: A suspension bridge supports its deck with steel cables hanging from two tall towers. The Golden Gate Bridge (San Francisco, opened 1937, designed by Joseph Strauss) spans 1,280 m and was the longest suspension bridge in the world for 27 years. The Brooklyn Bridge (1883, John A. Roebling) was the first major American suspension bridge using steel cables. NGSS MS-ETS1-2 expects students to recognize structural designs, compare strengths and trade-offs of bridge types, and credit famous engineering achievements. NYS Grade 6 Science tests bridge types and bridge engineers.

questions.prompt: Year the Wright brothers achieved the first powered, sustained, controlled airplane flight.
questions.explanation: On December 17, 1903 at Kitty Hawk, North Carolina, Wilbur and Orville Wright flew the Wright Flyer—the first powered, sustained, and controlled airplane flight. The first hop lasted 12 seconds and 36 meters; the day's longest was 59 seconds and 260 meters. The brothers iterated from 1900 through 1903, building wind tunnels to test wing shapes (over 200 airfoil designs). They pioneered three-axis control (pitch, roll, yaw)—still used in every aircraft today. NYS Grade 6 Science (NGSS MS-ETS1-4) requires crediting Wright brothers 1903 as iterative design exemplar.

questions.prompt: Engineer behind both the Brooklyn Bridge and the steel-cable suspension method that made it possible.
questions.explanation: John Augustus Roebling (1806-1869), a German-American engineer, perfected the steel-cable suspension bridge and designed the Brooklyn Bridge—opened in 1883, the longest suspension bridge in the world at the time at 486 m. He patented the wire-rope manufacturing process in 1842. After his death from tetanus in 1869 (from a foot injury at the bridge site), his son Washington Roebling completed construction with his wife Emily helping run the project. NYS Grade 6 Science (NGSS MS-ETS1-2) honors Roebling as exemplar of design-iteration leadership in civil engineering.

questions.prompt: Iron-and-steel tower built for the 1889 Paris World's Fair that demonstrated wind-load engineering.
questions.explanation: The Eiffel Tower in Paris was designed by Gustave Eiffel's engineering firm (chief engineers Maurice Koechlin and Emile Nouguier) and built for the 1889 World's Fair—celebrating the centennial of the French Revolution. At 330 m tall, it was the world's tallest structure for 41 years (until the 1930 Chrysler Building). Its lattice design distributes wind loads efficiently—a textbook example of structural engineering. Eiffel also designed the framework inside the Statue of Liberty. NYS Grade 6 Science (NGSS MS-ETS1-2) features the Eiffel Tower as an engineering icon.

questions.prompt: Smartphone Steve Jobs unveiled in 2007 that combined a phone, music player, and Internet browser.
questions.explanation: Steve Jobs unveiled the iPhone on January 9, 2007 at the Macworld Conference & Expo, calling it 'an iPod, a phone, and an Internet communicator.' Released June 29, 2007, the iPhone fused three categories into one device with a capacitive multi-touch screen (developed at Apple by Jonathan Ive's team based on FingerWorks tech acquired in 2005). Over 2.3 billion iPhones have sold through 2024. NGSS MS-ETS1-4 features the iPhone as a textbook iterative-design success: each year refines hardware and software, with hundreds of small improvements per release.

questions.prompt: Three-part principle that all living things are made of cells, the cell is the basic unit of life, and cells come from cells.
questions.explanation: Cell theory has three tenets: (1) all living things are made of cells (Schleiden 1838, Schwann 1839), (2) the cell is the basic structural and functional unit of life, and (3) all cells arise from pre-existing cells (Rudolf Virchow 1855). It unifies the biology of every domain from bacteria to whales. NYS Grade 6 Science (NGSS MS-LS1-1) requires students to list all three tenets and credit each scientist; cumulative assessments may ask for both timing (1838-1855) and the modern addition that cells contain hereditary DNA.

questions.prompt: Cell organelle that produces ATP from glucose and oxygen, called the 'powerhouse.'
questions.explanation: Mitochondria are bean-shaped organelles in eukaryotic cells where cellular respiration converts glucose plus oxygen into CO2, water, and about 36 ATP per glucose. They have their own DNA and double membrane, evidence for Lynn Margulis's 1967 endosymbiotic theory. Muscle and heart cells, which need much energy, contain thousands of mitochondria each. NYS Grade 6 Science (NGSS MS-LS1-2, MS-LS1-7) requires cumulative-test students to link mitochondria to cellular respiration AND identify them as descendants of ancient bacteria.

questions.prompt: Tenth percent of energy that transfers from one trophic level to the next in a food chain.
questions.explanation: The 10% rule, formalized by ecologist Raymond Lindeman in his landmark 1942 paper The Trophic-Dynamic Aspect of Ecology, states that about 10% of energy at one trophic level transfers to the next. The other 90% is lost as heat in cellular respiration plus growth, movement, and waste. This limits most food chains to about 4-5 trophic levels and explains why apex predators are rare. NYS Grade 6 Science (NGSS MS-LS2-3) requires students to apply the 10% rule and credit Lindeman 1942 on cumulative assessments.

questions.prompt: Process where rising water vapor cools and forms cloud droplets, releasing latent heat that powers storms.
questions.explanation: Condensation is the phase change of water vapor back to liquid as rising air cools at the adiabatic lapse rate (~6.5 C/km) until it hits dew point. It releases about 2,260 kJ of latent heat per kilogram of water—the primary energy source for thunderstorms and hurricanes. Luke Howard named the basic cloud forms (cumulus, stratus, cirrus) in 1803. NYS Grade 6 Science (NGSS MS-ESS2-4, MS-ESS2-5) requires cumulative-test students to link condensation, latent heat, and storm energy and credit Howard 1803 for cloud naming.

questions.prompt: Equation in which 6 CO2 + 6 H2O + light yields C6H12O6 + 6 O2.
questions.explanation: Photosynthesis is the central energy-input reaction for nearly all life: 6 CO2 + 6 H2O + light energy -> C6H12O6 + 6 O2, in plant chloroplasts. Jan Ingenhousz showed in 1779 that sunlight is essential; Melvin Calvin used carbon-14 from 1946-1956 to trace the path of CO2 fixation (Nobel 1961). Photosynthesis produces nearly all atmospheric O2 and forms the base of nearly every food chain. NYS Grade 6 Science (NGSS MS-LS1-6) requires writing the equation and crediting Ingenhousz on cumulative assessments.

questions.prompt: Reverse equation of photosynthesis run by mitochondria to extract energy from sugar.
questions.explanation: Cellular respiration runs photosynthesis in reverse: C6H12O6 + 6 O2 -> 6 CO2 + 6 H2O + ~36 ATP. It occurs in mitochondria (Hans Krebs's citric-acid cycle 1937; Peter Mitchell's chemiosmotic electron transport 1961). Antoine Lavoisier in 1789 first showed respiration is slow combustion using oxygen. Together with photosynthesis it forms the matter-and-energy cycle that sustains all aerobic life. NYS Grade 6 Science (NGSS MS-LS1-7) requires cumulative-test students to write both equations and credit Lavoisier 1789 and Krebs 1937.

questions.prompt: Carl Linnaeus's 1735 method of categorizing all life into kingdoms, classes, orders, families, genera, and species.
questions.explanation: Carl Linnaeus, a Swedish botanist, published Systema Naturae in 1735 introducing hierarchical taxonomy: kingdom, phylum, class, order, family, genus, species. He also introduced binomial nomenclature—giving each species a two-word Latin name (Homo sapiens for humans). Linnaean taxonomy organized the chaos of pre-1735 biology and remains the foundation, though molecular phylogenetics (Carl Woese 1977, three domains) now refines it. NYS Grade 6 Science (NGSS MS-LS1-1) requires cumulative students to credit Linnaeus 1735.

questions.prompt: Dmitri Mendeleev's 1869 arrangement of elements that predicts properties from atomic number.
questions.explanation: Dmitri Mendeleev, a Russian chemist, published the first periodic table in 1869, arranging the 63 then-known elements by atomic mass and recurring chemical properties. He left gaps for undiscovered elements (gallium 1875, scandium 1879, germanium 1886) and correctly predicted their properties—a triumph of organized science. Henry Moseley refined the ordering by atomic number in 1913. NYS Grade 6 Science (NGSS MS-PS1-1) requires cumulative-test students to credit Mendeleev 1869 and identify the periodic table as a predictive tool.

questions.prompt: Antoine Lavoisier's 1789 law that mass is neither created nor destroyed in a chemical reaction.
questions.explanation: The law of conservation of mass, established by Antoine Lavoisier in his 1789 Traite Elementaire de Chimie, states that in a closed system the total mass of reactants equals the total mass of products. Lavoisier proved this by carefully weighing combustion experiments, also disproving the phlogiston theory and identifying oxygen. He was guillotined in 1794 during the French Revolution. NYS Grade 6 Science (NGSS MS-PS1-5) requires cumulative-test students to credit Lavoisier 1789 and apply conservation to balance equations.

questions.prompt: Maintenance of stable internal conditions despite outside change, like 37 C body temperature.
questions.explanation: Homeostasis is the maintenance of stable internal conditions despite outside environmental change. Walter Cannon coined the term in his 1932 book The Wisdom of the Body, building on Claude Bernard's 1865 milieu interieur. Examples: body temperature near 37 C, blood glucose near 90 mg/dL, water and pH balance via the kidneys. NYS Grade 6 Science (NGSS MS-LS1-8) requires that cumulative-test students name homeostasis, give two examples, and credit Walter Cannon 1932 as the originator of the modern concept.

questions.prompt: Muscular pump that beats about 100,000 times each day to circulate blood.
questions.explanation: The heart is a fist-sized four-chambered muscular pump that beats about 100,000 times a day, moving roughly 7,500 liters of blood. William Harvey demonstrated in his 1628 book De Motu Cordis that blood circulates in a closed loop, overturning 1,400 years of Galen's medicine. The four chambers (two atria, two ventricles) keep oxygen-rich and oxygen-poor blood separate. NYS Grade 6 Science (NGSS MS-LS1-3) requires cumulative-test students to identify the heart, name its chambers, and credit Harvey 1628 for proving closed circulation.

questions.prompt: Tiny air sacs in the lungs where oxygen diffuses into the blood.
questions.explanation: Alveoli are about 300 million microscopic air sacs in the lungs where oxygen diffuses into capillary blood across a one-cell-thick membrane. Italian anatomist Marcello Malpighi first observed them under a microscope in 1661. Total alveolar surface area is about 70 square meters—the size of a tennis court. Smoking destroys alveolar walls (emphysema). NYS Grade 6 Science (NGSS MS-LS1-3) requires cumulative-test students to identify alveoli, link the enormous surface area to efficient O2 uptake, and credit Malpighi 1661.

questions.prompt: Process where a hand jerks off a hot stove before the brain consciously feels pain.
questions.explanation: A reflex is an automatic involuntary response routed through the spinal cord WITHOUT first going to the brain. British neurophysiologist Charles Sherrington traced the reflex arc in 1906 (Nobel Prize 1932): receptor -> sensory neuron -> spinal interneuron -> motor neuron -> effector muscle. Total time is under 50 milliseconds. The brain is informed AFTER the muscle has moved. NYS Grade 6 Science (NGSS MS-LS1-8) requires cumulative-test students to use the reflex arc to explain rapid response and credit Sherrington 1906.

questions.prompt: Iron-containing protein in red blood cells that binds and transports oxygen.
questions.explanation: Hemoglobin is the iron-containing protein inside red blood cells (erythrocytes) that binds and transports oxygen—four O2 molecules per hemoglobin via four heme groups. Austrian-British scientist Max Perutz solved its 3D structure in 1959 using X-ray crystallography (Nobel Prize 1962). Oxygen-rich (arterial) blood appears bright red; oxygen-poor (venous) blood is darker. NYS Grade 6 Science (NGSS MS-LS1-3) requires cumulative-test students to name hemoglobin, link low iron intake to anemia, and credit Perutz 1959.

questions.prompt: Eight planets orbiting the Sun since this body was reclassified as a dwarf planet in 2006.
questions.explanation: Pluto was reclassified as a dwarf planet by the International Astronomical Union (IAU) on August 24, 2006, after the discovery of Eris (a similar-sized Kuiper-belt body) forced a precise definition: a planet must (1) orbit the Sun, (2) be spherical from its own gravity, and (3) have cleared its orbital neighborhood of debris. Pluto fails the third test. NYS Grade 6 Science (NGSS MS-ESS1-3) requires students to know the IAU 2006 ruling and the resulting eight-planet solar-system count on cumulative assessments.

questions.prompt: Polish astronomer who in 1543 proposed the Sun-centered (heliocentric) model.
questions.explanation: Nicolaus Copernicus (1473-1543), a Polish canon and astronomer, published De Revolutionibus Orbium Coelestium in 1543—the year of his death—proposing a heliocentric (Sun-centered) model of the solar system. Galileo's 1610 telescopic discoveries and Kepler's 1609 elliptical-orbit laws confirmed Copernicus. Newton's 1687 gravity law explained why orbits work. NYS Grade 6 Science (NGSS MS-ESS1-3) requires cumulative-test students to credit Copernicus 1543 and trace the lineage Copernicus -> Galileo -> Kepler -> Newton.

questions.prompt: Three German-born scientists whose three laws of motion describe planetary orbits as ellipses.
questions.explanation: Johannes Kepler (1571-1630), German astronomer to Emperor Rudolf II, used Tycho Brahe's precise observations of Mars to formulate three laws of planetary motion: (1) orbits are ellipses with the Sun at one focus (1609), (2) equal areas swept in equal times (1609), (3) period squared proportional to semi-major axis cubed (1619). Kepler's laws made Copernican astronomy quantitatively predictive. Newton 1687 explained them with gravity. NYS Grade 6 Science (NGSS MS-ESS1-3) requires crediting Kepler on cumulative tests.

questions.prompt: Theory that the universe began about 13.8 billion years ago in a hot dense expansion.
questions.explanation: The Big Bang theory states the universe began about 13.8 billion years ago in a hot dense state and has been expanding ever since. Belgian priest-physicist Georges Lemaitre proposed the 'primeval atom' in 1927, Edwin Hubble measured galactic redshift in 1929, and Arno Penzias and Robert Wilson detected the cosmic microwave background in 1964 (Nobel 1978). The CMB (now 2.7 K) is the residual heat of the Big Bang. NYS Grade 6 Science (NGSS MS-ESS1-2) requires the 13.8 billion year age value and three credits.

questions.prompt: Earth's 23.5-degree axial tilt is the cause of these annual climate cycles.
questions.explanation: Seasons result from Earth's 23.5-degree axial tilt: in June the Northern Hemisphere points toward the Sun (summer) while the Southern Hemisphere points away (winter); in December it reverses. The tilt also defines the Tropic of Cancer and Tropic of Capricorn (where the Sun is overhead at solstice) and the Arctic and Antarctic Circles. NYS Grade 6 Science (NGSS MS-ESS1-1) requires cumulative-test students to attribute seasons to axial tilt (NOT distance from Sun) and identify 23.5 degrees as the tilt angle.

questions.prompt: Long-term increase in Earth's average temperature, mainly from burning fossil fuels.
questions.explanation: Global warming is the multi-decadal rise in Earth's average temperature, driven primarily by anthropogenic CO2 strengthening the greenhouse effect. Joseph Fourier proposed the mechanism in 1824, Eunice Foote demonstrated CO2 heat-trapping in 1856, John Tyndall confirmed in 1859, Svante Arrhenius calculated in 1896 that doubling CO2 would warm Earth ~5 C. The Keeling Curve (since 1958) and IPCC reports (since 1990) provide cumulative evidence. NYS Grade 6 Science (NGSS MS-ESS3-5) requires students to credit all five scientists.

questions.prompt: Three-part process by which species adapt over generations: variation, selection, inheritance.
questions.explanation: Natural selection, the mechanism of evolution proposed by Charles Darwin and Alfred Russel Wallace in 1858 and detailed in Darwin's On the Origin of Species in 1859, requires (1) heritable variation in a trait, (2) differential survival/reproduction based on that trait, and (3) inheritance of the favored trait to offspring. Over many generations, populations adapt. Gregor Mendel's 1866 genetics work supplied the inheritance mechanism. NYS Grade 6 Science (NGSS MS-LS4-4) requires crediting Darwin-Wallace 1858 and Mendel 1866.

questions.prompt: Atomic-scale unit of inheritance that Wilhelm Johannsen named in 1909.
questions.explanation: Wilhelm Johannsen, Danish botanist, coined the term gene in 1909 to name the discrete heritable units Mendel had called factors in 1866. Genes are sections of DNA on chromosomes that code for one trait or protein. Watson and Crick described DNA's double helix in 1953; the Human Genome Project finished in 2003 sequencing all ~20,000 human genes. NYS Grade 6 Science (NGSS MS-LS3-2) requires cumulative-test students to define gene, credit Johannsen 1909 for the term, and credit Watson-Crick 1953 for DNA structure.

questions.prompt: Engineering's six-step iterative cycle of design, build, test, and improve.
questions.explanation: The engineering design process: (1) define the problem with criteria/constraints, (2) brainstorm solutions, (3) select and prototype, (4) test, (5) iterate, (6) communicate. NGSS Engineering Practices and MS-ETS1-1 through MS-ETS1-4 require all six steps. The Wright brothers (1900-1903), SpaceX (2002-present), and Apple's iPhone (2007-present) all exemplify iterative design. NYS Grade 6 Science requires cumulative-test students to order all six steps and identify iteration as the heart of engineering.

questions.prompt: Three-tenet 1855 principle finalized by Schleiden, Schwann, and Virchow.
questions.explanation: Cell theory was finalized in 1855 by Rudolf Virchow's statement 'Omnis cellula e cellula' (every cell from a cell), building on Schleiden's 1838 plant work and Schwann's 1839 animal work. The three tenets: all living things are made of cells, the cell is the basic unit of life, all cells come from pre-existing cells. Modern additions include hereditary DNA and energy flow within cells. NYS Grade 6 Science (NGSS MS-LS1-1) requires cumulative-test students to list all three tenets and credit each scientist.
